Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] in 397. The name of [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]] originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of [[:en:Austronesian peoples|Austronesian]] origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as [[:en:Tagalog language|Tagalog]] ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and [[:en:Malay language|Malay]] and [[:en:Indonesian language|Indonesian]] ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in [[:en:Austronesian languages|Austronesian languages]].Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * [[:en:Bear worship|Bear worship]] * [[:en:Hayato people|Hayato people]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196897]] Wikidata name: Kumaso [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Historical ethnic group inhabiting southern Kyūshū}} [[File:Yamato Takerunomikoto & Kawakami Takeru.jpg|thumb|Prince Yamato Takeru attacking Kawakami Takeru (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}})]] The {{Nihongo|'''Kumaso'''|熊襲||}} were a mythical people of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'',角林, 文雄「隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族」、『京都産業大学日本文化研究所紀要』第3号、京都産業大学、1998th year 3月、 ISSN {{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&q=hayato+people+austronesian&pg=PA368|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|last=Kidder|first=Jonathan Edward|date=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=9780824830359|language=en}} believed to have lived in the south of [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]]{{cite wikisource |last1=Aston |first1=W. G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1196897]] Wikidata name: Kumaso [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Historical ethnic group inhabiting southern Kyūshū}} [[File:Yamato Takerunomikoto & Kawakami Takeru.jpg|thumb|Prince Yamato Takeru attacking Kawakami Takeru (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}})]] The {{Nihongo|'''Kumaso'''|熊襲||}} were a mythical people of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'',角林, 文雄「隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族」、『京都産業大学日本文化研究所紀要』第3号、京都産業大学、1998th year 3月、 ISSN {{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&q=hayato+people+austronesian&pg=PA368|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|last=Kidder|first=Jonathan Edward|date=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=9780824830359|language=en}} believed to have lived in the south of [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]]{{cite wikisource |last1=Aston |first1=W. G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
[[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] {{Short description|Historical ethnic group inhabiting southern Kyūshū}} [[File:Yamato Takerunomikoto & Kawakami Takeru.jpg|thumb|Prince Yamato Takeru attacking Kawakami Takeru (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|1=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}})]] The {{Nihongo|'''Kumaso'''|熊襲||}} were a mythical people of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'',角林, 文雄「隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族」、『京都産業大学日本文化研究所紀要』第3号、京都産業大学、1998th year 3月、 ISSN {{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&q=hayato+people+austronesian&pg=PA368|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|last=Kidder|first=Jonathan Edward|date=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=9780824830359|language=en}} believed to have lived in the south of [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]]{{cite wikisource |last1=Aston |first1=W. G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian|1=Austronesian peoples}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog|1=Tagalog language}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay|1=Malay language}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian|1=Indonesian language}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages|1=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin|1=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district|1=Soo District, Kagoshima}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto|1=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship|1=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people|1=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship|1=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people|1=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] [[en:Kumaso]][[es:Kumaso]][[fa:کوماسو]][[fr:Kumaso]][[id:Kumaso]][[ja:熊襲]][[ko:구마소]][[pt:Kumaso]][[ru:Кумасо]][[uk:Кумасо]][[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]][[zh:熊襲]] {{Short description|Historical ethnic group inhabiting southern Kyūshū}} [[File:Yamato Takerunomikoto & Kawakami Takeru.jpg|thumb|Prince Yamato Takeru attacking Kawakami Takeru (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|1=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}})]] The {{Nihongo|'''Kumaso'''|熊襲||}} were a mythical people of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]'',角林, 文雄「隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族」、『京都産業大学日本文化研究所紀要』第3号、京都産業大学、1998th year 3月、 ISSN {{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7pEOicqsjv0C&q=hayato+people+austronesian&pg=PA368|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|last=Kidder|first=Jonathan Edward|date=2007|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=9780824830359|language=en}} believed to have lived in the south of [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]]{{cite wikisource |last1=Aston |first1=W. G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|WD=Q861225|lt=Austronesian|1=Austronesian peoples}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|WD=Q34057|lt=Tagalog|1=Tagalog language}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|WD=Q9237|lt=Malay|1=Malay language}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|WD=Q9240|lt=Indonesian|1=Indonesian language}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|WD=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages|1=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|WD=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin|1=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|WD=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|WD=Q1043870|lt=So district|1=Soo District, Kagoshima}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|WD=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|WD=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|WD=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto|1=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 201. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Atsukaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Sakaya: a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 195. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-fukaya: {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}} married her 82 AD and in the same year put her to death, since she was involved in the assassination which killed her father.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} *Ichi-kaya: younger sister of Ichi-fukaya''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 196. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} {{Wiktionary pipe|熊襲|kumaso}} ==See also== * {{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|WD=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship|1=Bear worship}} * {{ill|Hayato people|en|Hayato people|WD=Q1151764|lt=Hayato people|1=Hayato people}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ethnic groups in Japan}} [[issn:13417207|13417207]] [[wikt:kaya#Tagalog|kaya]] [[wikt:kaya#Malay|kaya]] [[wikt:tao#Tagalog|tao]] [[en:Kumaso]] [[es:Kumaso]] [[fa:کوماسو]] [[fr:Kumaso]] [[id:Kumaso]] [[ja:熊襲]] [[ko:구마소]] [[pt:Kumaso]] [[ru:Кумасо]] [[uk:Кумасо]] [[zh:熊襲]] [[zh-min-nan:Kumaso]] {{wikidata link|Q1196897}} [[Category:Tribes of ancient Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

G. |authorlink1=William George Aston |date=1896 |chapter=Book VII |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=[[Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.]] |at=p. 192, note 3 |scan=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/223 |quote=The country of the Kumaso was the southern part of the island of Kiushiu corresponding to the present provinces of Hiuga, Ohosumi, and Satsuma. Kuma and So are the names of two tribes.}} until at least the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya was killed by {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} in 397. The name of {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} originates from the Kumaso people. == Hypothesized Origin == Scholars, such as Kakubayashi Fumio, "although information is extremely limited" concluded that they were of {{ill|Austronesian peoples|en|Austronesian peoples|qid=Q861225|lt=Austronesian|1=Austronesian peoples}} origin based on some linguistic and cultural evidence, theorising that the word ''kaya'', present in personal names or titles, such as Torishi-Kaya, has the same root as {{ill|Tagalog language|en|Tagalog language|qid=Q34057|lt=Tagalog|1=Tagalog language}} ''""'', meaning "ability; capability; competence; resources; wealth" and {{ill|Malay language|en|Malay language|qid=Q9237|lt=Malay|1=Malay language}} and {{ill|Indonesian language|en|Indonesian language|qid=Q9240|lt=Indonesian|1=Indonesian language}} ''""'', meaning "rich, wealthy, having wealth". The ''So'' present in Kumaso was also theorised to have the same origins as ''tsuo, tsau, thau, sau, tau, '' supposedly meaning "people" in {{ill|Austronesian languages|en|Austronesian languages|qid=Q49228|lt=Austronesian languages|1=Austronesian languages}}.Kakubayashi, Fumio. 隼人 : オーストロネシア系の古代日本部族' Hayato : An Austronesian speaking tribe in southern Japan.'. The bulletin of the Institute for Japanese Culture, Kyoto Sangyo University, 3, pp.15-31 ISSN 1341-7207 {{ill|Alexander Vovin|en|Alexander Vovin|qid=Q848595|lt=Alexander Vovin|1=Alexander Vovin}} defended that the question of whether the Kumaso language was not Japonic is moot because not a single word of it is attested.{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2024-03-18 |work=Handbook of Historical Japanese Linguistics |pages=59 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9781614512851-toc |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=De Gruyter |isbn=978-1-61451-285-1}} Him and Mark Hudson also theorised there is a probability that the Kumaso and the Hayato represented the same ethnic group.{{Citation |title=Frontmatter |date=2017-12-31 |work=Ruins of Identity |pages=i–iv |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824864194-fm |access-date=2024-11-23 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6419-4}} == Overview == [[William George Aston]], in his translation of the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]'', says ''Kumaso'' refers to two separate tribes, ''Kuma'' (meaning "bear") and ''So'' (written with the character for "attack" or "layer on"). In his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]'', {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|qid=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} records that the region is also known simply as ''{{ill|Soo District, Kagoshima|en|Soo District, Kagoshima|qid=Q1043870|lt=So district|1=Soo District, Kagoshima}}'', and elaborates on the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|qid=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}}-centric description of a "bear-like" people, based on their violent interactions or physical distinctiveness.{{cite wikisource |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil Hall |authorlink1=Basil Hall Chamberlain |date=1882 |chapter=Sect. V |title=Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=[[Asiatic Society of Japan]] |at=p. 23, note 17 |scan=Page:Kojiki_by_Chamberlain.djvu/109 |quote=''Toyo'' means "luxuriant" or "fertile." ''Hi'' appears to signify "fire" or "sun." ''Kumaso'' is properly a compound, ''Kuma-so'', as the district is often mentioned by the simple name of ''So''. ''Kuma'' signifies "bear," and Motowori suggests that the use of the name of this the fiercest of beasts as a prefix may be traced to the evil reputation of that part of the country for robbers and outlaws. He quotes similar compounds with kuma in support of this view.}}
http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm#fn_173 (The people called ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|qid=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}'' by the Yamato people provide a better-known example of the transformation of other tribes into legendary monsters. ''Tsuchigumo''—the monstrous "ground spider" of legend—is speculated to refer originally to the native pit dwellings of that people.) Geographically, Aston records that the Kumaso domain encompassed the historical provinces of [[:en:Hyūga province|Hyūga]], [[:en:Ōsumi province|Ōsumi]], and [[:en:Satsuma province|Satsuma]] (contemporaneous with Aston's translation), or present-day [[Miyazaki prefecture|Miyazaki]] and [[:en:Kagoshima prefecture|Kagoshima]] prefectures. The last leader of the Kumaso, Torishi-Kaya, aka Brave of Kahakami, was assassinated in the winter of 397 by Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} of Yamato,''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book VII, page 200ff. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}} who was disguised for this as a woman at a banquet. == Legacy == The word Kuma ('Bear') survives today as {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} ('source of the bear'), and {{ill|Kuma District, Kumamoto|en|Kuma District, Kumamoto|qid=Q1132476|lt=Kuma District, Kumamoto|1=Kuma District, Kumamoto}}. Kuma District is known for a distinct dialect, Kuma Dialect. ==People of the Kumaso mentioned in the Nihongi== *Torishi-Kaya (aka Brave of Kahakami): a leader of the Kumaso''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]].
